notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your credit card balances show zero across both connected cards — your American Express Gold Card (1005) and Chase Freedom Unlimited (2094) — which most likely reflects a data timing issue rather than actual zero balances.
Here's why I'd flag this before drawing any conclusions:
All six of your accounts were connected manually and carry a "balance status: IDLE" flag, meaning no live balance data has been synced. The accounts were added on June 5th (today), so balances simply haven't populated yet. This is very common with manually connected accounts in the early hours after setup.
What to verify before optimizing anything
Step one — confirm actual card balances. Log directly into your American Express and Chase accounts to pull your current statement balances and any pending charges. Your May transactions show regular usage on both cards — $78.35 at Blue Pan Pizza and $86.40 at Snooze AM Eatery on the Amex Gold, plus grocery runs at Costco and King Soopers, Target, Peloton, Disney+, and Spotify on the Chase Freedom. These charges are real, so a balance should exist.
Step two — check whether balances are paid in full monthly. Based on your spending patterns, you appear to be using these cards responsibly for everyday purchases, which is a smart approach for rewards earning. If you're clearing the statement balance each cycle, there's no interest concern at all — just pending charges accumulating toward your next statement.
Step three — reconcile your net worth snapshot. All six accounts are currently showing $0 balances, including your Chase checking, two 401(k) plans, HSA, and both cards. Once balances sync properly, your net worth picture will be more accurate and we can give you much more meaningful guidance on your overall financial health.
Step four — reconnect or manually enter balances. If the idle status persists, entering your current balances manually in Origin will unlock better tracking and budgeting analysis right away.
The good news from your May transaction history is that your cash flow looks genuinely strong — $18,650 in income against $9,256 in expenses created nearly a $9,400 net surplus last month. Once your account balances are fully synced, would you like to dig into how that surplus is being allocated across savings, retirement, and other goals?
